masthead.dek: Questions on how we figured things out and built them — routed to a country, a decade, or a count so you reason rather than recall a name. Two engines drive most of them: 'older than you think' and 'not from where you'd think.' Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Verified to Britannica, NASA, Nobel.org, the Computer History Museum, and Nature. Time-routed questions never state the year — you derive the decade. A few answers (Soviet space firsts, UK medical firsts) recur, capped at twice each.

dimensions: most abundant element in the body
plan.note: Routes every answer to a country, decade, or count — never 'which scientist.' Chronology questions hide the year so the solver derives the decade. 'Older than you think' and 'not from where you'd think' are the recurring reflex-defeaters.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · fax older than telephone
questions.q: A working machine for sending a copied image over a wire was first patented in roughly which decade — the same kind of device we'd later call a fax?
questions.detail: Alexander Bain patented an image-scanning telegraph in 1843, 33 years before the telephone.
questions.why: You anchor on 'fax is a 20th-century gadget,' then realize scanning-over-a-wire is just telegraphy.
questions.more: A commercial Paris–Lyon fax service ran by 1865.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · ancient vending machine
questions.q: The first machine that gave you a product when you dropped in a coin was built in roughly which era — for dispensing holy water in a temple?
questions.detail: Hero of Alexandria's coin-operated holy-water dispenser used a coin's weight to tip a lever and open a valve.
questions.why: 'Vending machine = modern,' but the mechanism is just a balance and a valve.
questions.more: It was built to stop worshippers taking more than they paid for.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · true origin of a name
questions.q: The digits we call 'Arabic numerals' were actually invented in which country?
questions.detail: The glyphs descend from Indian Brahmi numerals; Arab mathematicians carried them west.
questions.why: The name is the trap; reason 'who invented vs who carried it.'
questions.more: Al-Khwarizmi's ~825 book described 'the numbers of the Indians.'

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first paper money
questions.q: The world's first true paper money — printed notes a government would honor — was issued in which country?
questions.detail: Song-dynasty 'jiaozi' became government-issued notes by the 1020s, centuries before European banknotes.
questions.why: Narrow to 'old + state-backed printed currency.'
questions.more: Tang-era 'flying money' was an earlier precursor.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · refugee inventor's patent
questions.q: In which country did the inventor of the modern ballpoint pen file his successful patent after fleeing Europe — a country where the pen still bears his name?
questions.detail: Laszlo Biro fled to Argentina and filed there in 1943; ballpoints are still called 'birome' there.
questions.why: Routing to the patent country defeats guessing the US or his native Hungary.
questions.more: He left Hungary the day before a law banned taking patents abroad.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · decimal point predates calc
questions.q: The decimal point in written numbers first shows up in which century — it predates the invention of calculus?
questions.detail: Giovanni Bianchini used a decimal point in tables in the 1440s, well before 1600s calculus.
questions.why: Weigh 'is the dot older or younger than calculus' — notation came first.
questions.more: Earlier credited use was Clavius in 1593.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first programmable computer
questions.q: The first working programmable, fully automatic digital computer (the Z3, 1941) was built in which country?
questions.detail: Konrad Zuse completed the relay-based Z3 in Berlin in 1941, before ENIAC.
questions.why: Reflex says US/UK; reason 'who finished a programmable digital machine first.'
questions.more: It used 2,600 relays and 22-bit words.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · wiper before the car boom
questions.q: The windshield wiper was patented in roughly which decade — so early that the Ford Model T hadn't even been built yet?
questions.detail: Mary Anderson patented a manual wiper in 1903; Cadillac made wipers standard only in 1922.
questions.why: Place the wiper before the car boom, not after it.
questions.more: She got the idea watching trolley drivers in the snow.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first text message
questions.q: The world's first SMS text message ('Merry Christmas') was sent in roughly which decade?
questions.detail: Neil Papworth sent the first SMS in December 1992 from a PC, before phones had keyboards.
questions.why: Texting feels tied to 2000s phones; push it back to the early cellular era.
questions.more: It was typed on a computer because the phone had no keyboard.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · oldest photograph
questions.q: The oldest surviving photograph, a rooftop view that took many hours to expose, was made in roughly which decade?
questions.detail: Niepce's 'View from the Window at Le Gras' dates to 1826–27, before Daguerre's 1839 process.
questions.why: Anchored on 1839, reason there was an earlier, cruder first image.
questions.more: The exposure ran for hours.
questions.source: Wikipedia (View from the Window at Le Gras).

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · telegraph before telephone
questions.q: The first commercial electric telegraph went into service in roughly which decade — well before the telephone existed?
questions.detail: Cooke and Wheatstone installed a commercial line on the Great Western Railway in 1838.
questions.why: Separate two wire technologies and place the telegraph first.
questions.more: Morse's famous US message came later, in 1844.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · can before the can opener
questions.q: Sealed canned food was patented in roughly which decade — so early that people opened the cans with a hammer and chisel for decades?
questions.detail: The tin can was patented in 1810; the can opener came ~50 years later.
questions.why: The 'hammer and chisel' gap forces you to date the can early.
questions.more: Driven by a French prize for feeding Napoleon's armies.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first program before comput
questions.q: The first algorithm written to be run by a machine (for Babbage's never-built engine) was published in roughly which decade?
questions.detail: Ada Lovelace published her Bernoulli-number algorithm in 1843, ~100 years before electronic computers.
questions.why: Resist 'no computer = no program' — the design and algorithm came first.
questions.more: She foresaw machines doing more than arithmetic.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · gunpowder's true purpose
questions.q: Gunpowder was first formulated in which country — by alchemists who were actually trying to brew an elixir of immortality?
questions.detail: Tang-dynasty Taoist alchemists (~9th c.) combined saltpeter, sulfur, and charcoal seeking the elixir of life.
questions.why: The 'immortality potion' detail confirms China and adds the irony.
questions.more: Early texts called it 'fire medicine.'

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · battery before the gadgets
questions.q: The first true battery — a stack of metal discs giving a steady current — was built in roughly which decade?
questions.detail: Volta's voltaic pile dates to 1800, decades before the telegraph it later helped power.
questions.why: Place a power source before the gadgets it would run.
questions.more: The 'Baghdad battery' is not accepted as a real battery.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first practical helicopter
questions.q: The first practical single-rotor helicopter (the VS-300) first flew in which country in 1939?
questions.detail: Igor Sikorsky, who emigrated from the Russian Empire, built and flew the VS-300 in Connecticut.
questions.why: Sikorsky's Russian origin is the trap; the machine was American.
questions.more: Its single main rotor plus tail rotor became the standard.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · the patent given away
questions.q: The modern three-point seatbelt was invented by a carmaker in which country, which then made the patent free for everyone?
questions.detail: Volvo engineer Nils Bohlin designed it in 1959, and Volvo opened the patent to all makers.
questions.why: The 'gave it away' detail pins the answer to Volvo's Sweden.
questions.more: It's credited with saving over a million lives.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first analog computer
questions.q: The Antikythera mechanism, the world's first known analog computer, was built by which ancient civilization (now a country)?
questions.detail: The bronze geared device (~150–100 BC) modeled the sun, moon, and planets and predicted eclipses.
questions.why: Accept that a 30+ gear computer is genuinely ancient Greek.
questions.more: Nothing as complex survives for over a thousand years afterward.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first industrial robot
questions.q: George Devol filed the patent for the first programmable industrial robot arm (Unimate) in roughly which decade?
questions.detail: Devol filed the Unimate patent in 1954; it began work on a GM line in 1961.
questions.why: Tempted by 1960s/70s, pull the patent back to the mid-1950s.
questions.more: Unimate stacked hot die-castings and welded car bodies.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · digital camera before consu
questions.q: The first self-contained digital camera (built at Kodak) was invented in roughly which decade?
questions.detail: Steven Sasson built it in 1975; it recorded 0.01-megapixel images to cassette tape.
questions.why: Separate 'invented' from 'sold' — push back to 1975.
questions.more: Affordable consumer digital cameras arrived only around 1994.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first webcam
questions.q: The world's first webcam, set up to watch a coffee pot, was built at a university in which country?
questions.detail: Cambridge researchers made the 'Trojan Room' coffee-pot camera in 1991; it went on the web in 1993.
questions.why: Reflex says US; reason 'where was the coffee-pot cam.'
questions.more: Its purpose was to spare scientists a wasted trip to an empty pot.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · Panama hat origin
questions.q: So-called 'Panama hats' are actually woven in which country?
questions.detail: The toquilla-straw hats are made in Ecuador and historically shipped via Panama, which stuck as the name.
questions.why: The name is the trap; reason about shipping vs making.
questions.more: A 1906 photo of Theodore Roosevelt in one cemented the misnomer.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first video game before Pon
questions.q: An interactive 'Tennis for Two' game, displayed on an oscilloscope for visitors, was built in roughly which decade — before Pong?
questions.detail: William Higinbotham built Tennis for Two at Brookhaven National Laboratory in 1958.
questions.why: Anchored on Pong/1970s, reach back to the 1950s.
questions.more: It ran on an analog computer made for plotting trajectories.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · Jacquard punch-card loom
questions.q: A loom that wove patterns from interchangeable punch cards — an early programmable machine — was demonstrated in roughly which decade?
questions.detail: Jacquard's punch-card loom was shown around 1801–04 in France; it later inspired Babbage.
questions.why: Connect 'punch cards' to weaving and place it a century before computers.
questions.more: Babbage borrowed the punch-card idea for his Analytical Engine.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first networked email
questions.q: The first networked email — introducing the @ in addresses — was sent in roughly which decade?
questions.detail: Ray Tomlinson sent it on ARPANET in 1971, choosing @ to mean a user 'at' another host.
questions.why: Separate ARPANET-era email from the 1990s web.
questions.more: The test message was roughly 'QWERTYUIOP.'

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first working laser
questions.q: The first working laser, built from a ruby rod, was demonstrated in roughly which decade?
questions.detail: Theodore Maiman operated the first ruby laser in May 1960 in California.
questions.why: Derive the decade without the year; many drift to the 1950s.
questions.more: Many doubted coherent light was even possible.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · most populous without a Nob
questions.q: Among the world's ten most populous countries, one has never had a single citizen win a Nobel Prize. Which country?
questions.detail: The world's 4th-most-populous nation has never produced a Nobel laureate.
questions.why: 'Most populous WITHOUT' defeats the reflex to name a tiny country.
questions.more: India, Pakistan, Nigeria, and Egypt all have at least one.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · closest planet on average
questions.q: Averaged over many years, which planet spends more time closer to Earth than any other?
questions.detail: Mercury's tight orbit keeps it nearest about 46% of the time, ahead of Venus and Mars.
questions.why: The reflex is Venus (closest single approach), but on average Mercury is nearest.
questions.more: By the same logic Mercury is closest on average to every planet.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · hottest planet
questions.q: Which planet has the hottest surface in the solar system, even though it is not the closest to the Sun?
questions.detail: Venus averages about 464°C because its dense CO2 atmosphere traps heat.
questions.why: The reflex is Mercury, but airless Mercury can't hold heat.
questions.more: Mercury averages only about 167°C.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · bones in an adult
questions.q: How many bones does a typical adult human skeleton have?
questions.detail: An adult has 206 bones; a newborn starts with around 300, many of which fuse.
questions.why: The surprise is that babies have MORE bones than adults.
questions.more: Fusion continues into the early 20s.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · bones in a hand
questions.q: How many bones are in a single human hand and wrist?
questions.detail: Eight carpal, five metacarpal, and fourteen phalange bones make 27 per hand.
questions.why: More than a quarter of all your bones are in your two hands.
questions.more: The thumb has two phalanges; each finger has three.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · smallest bone location
questions.q: In which part of the body is the smallest bone in the human skeleton found?
questions.detail: The stapes, only 2–3 mm long, sits in the middle ear and helps transmit sound.
questions.why: People reflexively guess a finger or toe.
questions.more: It's one of three tiny ossicles.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · most abundant element in th
questions.dimension: most abundant element in the body
questions.q: Which chemical element makes up the largest share of the human body by mass?
questions.detail: Oxygen is about 65% of body mass, mostly locked in water; carbon is second at ~18%.
questions.why: 'Carbon-based life' makes carbon the reflex, but water's oxygen outweighs it.
questions.more: Hydrogen and nitrogen round out the top four.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · number of basic tastes
questions.q: How many basic tastes does the human tongue detect, now that scientists include the savory one?
questions.detail: Sweet, salty, sour, bitter, and umami are the five recognized basic tastes.
questions.why: Many stop at four; umami joins as the fifth.
questions.more: The whole tongue senses all five; the 'taste map' is a myth.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · most moons
questions.q: As of recent counts, which planet has the most confirmed moons?
questions.detail: Saturn leapt past Jupiter after astronomers confirmed scores of new moons, giving it well over 270.
questions.why: Jupiter held the record for decades, but Saturn overtook it in the 2020s.
questions.more: Jupiter now sits at roughly 100 confirmed moons.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · sunlight travel time
questions.q: About how many minutes does sunlight take to travel from the Sun to Earth?
questions.detail: Light takes roughly 8 minutes 20 seconds to cross the ~150 million km.
questions.why: People guess instant, seconds, or hours; the scale is about eight minutes.
questions.more: You always see the Sun as it looked eight minutes ago.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first artificial satellite
questions.q: Which country launched the first artificial satellite to orbit Earth?
questions.detail: Sputnik 1, launched in 1957, was the first human-made object to orbit Earth.
questions.why: Many assume the US led every space first; the Soviets got there first.
questions.more: The launch spurred the creation of NASA.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first object on the Moon
questions.q: Which country first put a human-made object onto the Moon's surface?
questions.detail: The Soviet probe Luna 2 impacted the Moon in 1959, a decade before any crewed landing.
questions.why: People tie 'first object on the Moon' to Apollo 11; a Soviet probe reached it ten years earlier.
questions.more: Luna 2 was the first object to touch another celestial body.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first heart transplant
questions.q: In which country was the world's first human-to-human heart transplant performed?
questions.detail: Christiaan Barnard performed the first heart transplant in Cape Town in 1967.
questions.why: People expect the US or Europe; it happened in South Africa.
questions.more: The patient survived 18 days.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first probe to Pluto
questions.q: Which country sent the first spacecraft to fly past Pluto?
questions.detail: NASA's New Horizons made the first-ever flyby of Pluto in 2015.
questions.why: Routes a space first away from the Soviets; the US is the only nation to reach Pluto.
questions.more: It also imaged Pluto's moon Charon.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first cloned mammal
questions.q: In which country was Dolly the sheep, the first mammal cloned from an adult cell, created?
questions.detail: Dolly was cloned at the Roslin Institute in Scotland in 1996.
questions.why: People may guess the US; it was a Scottish lab.
questions.more: She was named after Dolly Parton.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first IVF baby
questions.q: In which country was the world's first IVF ('test-tube') baby born?
questions.detail: Louise Brown, the first baby conceived by IVF, was born in England in 1978.
questions.why: A reproductive-medicine first many assume was American.
questions.more: Robert Edwards later won a Nobel Prize for developing IVF.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first periodic table
questions.q: In which country was the first widely used periodic table of the elements created?
questions.detail: Dmitri Mendeleev published his periodic table in Russia in 1869, leaving gaps for undiscovered elements.
questions.why: Routes to a country rather than a scientist; many forget Mendeleev was Russian.
questions.more: His gaps were filled by gallium, scandium, and germanium.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · discovery of X-rays
questions.q: In which country were X-rays discovered in the 1890s?
questions.detail: Wilhelm Roentgen discovered X-rays at the University of Wurzburg in 1895.
questions.why: Routes to a country; the discovery earned the first-ever Nobel Prize in Physics.
questions.more: His first X-ray image showed his wife's hand and ring.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · decade the genome was compl
questions.q: In which decade was the Human Genome Project's sequence first completed?
questions.detail: The project announced completion in 2003 after a 13-year effort.
questions.why: People assume it was more recent; the draft was done in the early 2000s.
questions.more: A truly gap-free sequence followed in 2022.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · metric system origin
questions.q: In which decade was the metric system first established, during a famous revolution?
questions.detail: France adopted the metric system in the 1790s, defining the meter during the Revolution.
questions.why: People assume it's 19th- or 20th-century; it dates to the 1790s.
questions.more: The meter was set as one ten-millionth of the pole-to-equator distance.

questions.cat: Science, discovery & invention · first smallpox vaccine
questions.q: In which country was the first vaccine, against smallpox, developed in the 1790s?
questions.detail: Edward Jenner developed the smallpox vaccine in England using cowpox in 1796.
questions.why: People underestimate how old vaccination is — before anyone knew about germs.
questions.more: 'Vaccine' comes from vacca, Latin for cow.
